content.problemStatement:
For a sequence XXX of length L (L≥3)L \ (L \geq 3)L (L≥3), we call XXX a **concussive sequence** if and only if:


For all i (1<i<L)i \ (1\lt i \lt L)i (1<i<L), Xi−1<Xi>Xi+1X_{i-1}\lt X_i \gt X_{i+1}Xi−1​<Xi​>Xi+1​ or Xi−1>Xi<Xi+1X_{i-1}\gt X_i \lt X_{i+1}Xi−1​>Xi​<Xi+1​ holds.
You're given a sequence AAA of length NNN.

Your task is to rearrange AAA to make it a **concussive sequence**.

If multiple such rearrangements exist, print any. If no such rearrangement exists, output −1−1−1 instead.


Input Format


The first line contains the number of test cases TTT.




The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the sequence.




The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, elements of the sequence.





Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, NNN space-separated integers denoting the rearrangement.


If multiple such rearrangements exist, print any. If no such rearrangement exists, output −1-1−1 instead.
constraints: 3 ≤ N ≤ 3·10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N (N ≥ 3), the length of the sequence. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line. If it is possible to rearrange the given numbers into a concussive (strictly alternating peak‑valley) sequence, print any such permutation as N space‑separated integers. If it is impossible, print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: all numbers equal, condition sorted[0] >= sorted[1] fails, so -1. Second: sorted array is [1,2,3,4,5]; k=2, 2<3 holds. Small=[1,2], Large=[3,4,5] → 3 1 4 2 5, which is a valid concussive sequence (any valid permutation accepted, sample shows 1 3 2 5 4). Third: sorted [1,1,1,2,2]; k=2, sorted[1]=1 < sorted[2]=1? false, but there exists a valid arrangement 1 2 1 2 1, which our algorithm also produces because after sorting condition holds (sorted[1]=1 < sorted[2]=2).
Sorted = [1,2,3,4]; k=2, 2<3 true. Small=[1,2], Large=[3,4] → 3 1 4 2, which satisfies 3>1<4>2.

solution.approach: For a strict alternating sequence we can think of the elements at even positions (1‑based) as peaks and the elements at odd positions as valleys (or vice‑versa). If we sort the whole multiset, let k = floor(N/2). The smallest k elements will become the valleys and the remaining elements the peaks. The construction works iff the largest valley is strictly smaller than the smallest peak, i.e. sorted[k‑1] < sorted[k]. If this condition holds we interleave: start with the first peak, then the first valley, then the second peak, etc. When N is odd the peak array has one extra element and it will appear at the end. This yields a sequence where every internal element is either a peak (greater than both neighbours) or a valley (smaller than both neighbours). If the condition fails, any permutation would contain two equal or non‑alternating neighbours, so the answer is -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case for sorting; overall O((ΣN) log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing and sorting the array.
